title: Nouns
description: Learn to identify common, proper, concrete, abstract, and collective nouns.

forms.positive: Singular: Noun (e.g., dog, city) | Plural: Noun + -s/-es (e.g., dogs, cities) or Irregular (e.g., children, people)
forms.question: Is the noun countable (many, few) or uncountable (much, little)?

usages.description: Common nouns name general things (lower case), while proper nouns name specific entities (capitalized).
examples: **Albert Einstein** developed the theory of relativity.

usages.description: Concrete nouns name physical things you can experience with your five senses, whereas abstract nouns name qualities, concepts, or emotions.
Collective nouns name groups of people, animals, or things viewed as a single unit.

commonMistakes.incorrect: The paper was full of interesting informations.
commonMistakes.correct: The paper was full of interesting information.
commonMistakes.explanation: 'Information' is an uncountable noun and cannot be made plural with -s. Use 'information' or 'pieces of information'.

commonMistakes.incorrect: I saw two childs playing outside.
commonMistakes.correct: I saw two children playing outside.
commonMistakes.explanation: 'Child' is an irregular plural noun. Its plural form is 'children', not 'childs'.

quizzes.question: Which of the following is a proper noun?
quizzes.explanation: Proper nouns name specific places and must be capitalized ('London').

quizzes.question: Identify the abstract noun: 'She showed great honesty when returning the wallet.'
quizzes.explanation: 'Honesty' is an abstract concept/quality that you cannot touch or see physically, making it an abstract noun.

quizzes.question: What is the correct plural form of the irregular noun 'tooth'?
quizzes.explanation: 'Tooth' changes internally to form the irregular plural 'teeth'.
